system_prompt: You are taking a pure ACCURACY benchmark for shell one-liners. Think as long as you need before answering — there is no speed component, only correctness. You get exactly ONE shot per task: your command will be executed once in a sandbox and its stdout compared BYTE-FOR-BYTE against a golden answer (only a single trailing newline is trimmed). There is no chance to run, test, or revise anything. Environment: macOS with BSD userland (/bin/bash, BSD sed/awk/sort/grep/wc/join/uniq/tr — NOT GNU; no gsed/gsort; grep has no -P), plus python3 and jq available. Each command runs with bash -c in a directory containing exactly the input files described in the task. Commands must be pure and deterministic: no network, no writing files, no reading the clock. Output EXACTLY 10 lines, one per task, each of the form `Qn: <command>` where <command> is the raw one-line shell command. No code fences, no backticks, no explanations, no blank lines — anything else is scored as wrong.

tasks.prompt:
The file sales.csv contains exactly these 6 lines:
fruit,apple,12.55
veg,carrot,7.25
fruit,banana,3.75
veg,onion,2.50
dairy,milk,16.30
fruit,cherry,0.00
Each line is category,item,amount. Print the total amount per category, one line per category, formatted as `category total` (single space separator) with the total rounded to exactly 2 decimal places. Sort lines by total DESCENDING; break ties by category name ASCENDING.
tasks.reference_command: awk -F, '{s[$1]+=$3} END{for(c in s) printf "%s %.2f\n", c, s[c]}' sales.csv | sort -k2,2nr -k1,1
tasks.trap: fruit and dairy tie at exactly 16.30; `sort -k2,2nr` (or -rn) without an explicit `-k1,1` secondary key leaves the tie order to sort's last-resort whole-line comparison, which under -r puts fruit before dairy — wrong. Also relying on awk's for-in ordering, or printing unformatted sums (16.3 instead of 16.30), fails the byte compare.

tasks.prompt:
Two files. products.csv contains exactly these 4 lines (NOT sorted):
p03,widget
p01,gadget
p02,doohickey
p05,gizmo
stock.csv contains exactly these 3 lines:
p01,42
p03,7
p05,0
For EVERY product, print `id,name,qty` (comma-separated, no spaces), where qty comes from stock.csv, or the literal string NA if the id has no stock line. Output sorted by id ASCENDING.
tasks.trap: join requires both inputs pre-sorted on the key — products.csv is not, so a bare `join products.csv stock.csv` silently drops/garbles rows. And `-e NA` only substitutes for missing fields when an explicit `-o` field list is given; `join -a1 -e NA` without `-o` prints `p02,doohickey` with no NA field.

tasks.prompt: The file words.txt has DOS (CRLF) line endings and contains exactly these 7 lines: Apple, BANANA, apple, Cherry, banana, CHERRY, date (each line terminated by \r\n). Print the lines deduplicated CASE-INSENSITIVELY, keeping only the FIRST occurrence of each word in its original spelling and original order. Output must use plain LF line endings with no carriage returns.
tasks.trap: Forgetting to strip \r yields output whose every line ends in an invisible CR — dedup still works but the byte compare fails. Alternatively `sort -uf` dedups case-insensitively but destroys original order and may keep the wrong spelling; BSD `uniq -i` only collapses adjacent lines.

tasks.prompt:
The file stats.txt contains exactly these 4 lines:
alice 2 5
bob 4 9
carol 2 7
dave 4 6
Each line is `name count total`. For each line, in input order, compute the average total/count and round it to the nearest INTEGER using HALF-UP rounding (x.5 always rounds up, e.g. 2.5 -> 3). Print one line per input line formatted `name avg`.
tasks.trap: The natural `printf "%.0f"` uses IEEE round-half-to-even: 5/2=2.5 prints as 2, not the required 3 (verified: BSD awk %.0f of 2.5 -> 2). python3's round() has the same banker's-rounding behavior. You must implement half-up explicitly, e.g. int(x+0.5).

tasks.prompt:
The file ledger.txt contains exactly these 6 lines:
# monthly ledger
2024-01-02 CREDIT 100.00
2024-01-05 DEBIT 42.35
# adjustment
2024-01-09 DEBIT 77.10
2024-01-12 CREDIT 5.00
Ignoring lines starting with #, maintain a running balance starting at 0 where CREDIT adds the amount and DEBIT subtracts it. For each transaction, in order, print `date balance` where balance is formatted with printf format %09.2f (2 decimals, zero-padded to a total field width of 9 characters, minus sign counted inside the width, e.g. -19.45 prints as -00019.45).
tasks.setup:
printf '# monthly ledger
2024-01-02 CREDIT 100.00
2024-01-05 DEBIT 42.35
# adjustment
2024-01-09 DEBIT 77.10
2024-01-12 CREDIT 5.00
' > ledger.txt
tasks.trap: The balance goes negative mid-stream: %09.2f puts the zeros AFTER the minus sign (-00019.45, 9 chars total). Hand-rolling the padding, using width 8, or space-padding gives a near-miss. Forgetting to skip the two # comment lines corrupts the running total via awk's uninitialized-field arithmetic.

tasks.prompt:
The file matrix.tsv is TAB-separated with exactly these 3 rows and 3 columns:
id\tname\tqty
r1\tblue pen\t3
r2\tred ink\t7
Note that some cells contain internal spaces (e.g. `blue pen` is ONE cell). Print the transpose of this matrix, TAB-separated: 3 rows where row i contains column i of the original (first output row: id, r1, r2). No trailing tabs.
tasks.reference_command: awk -F'\t' '{for(i=1;i<=NF;i++) r[i]=(NR==1 ? $i : r[i] "\t" $i)} END{for(i=1;i<=NF;i++) print r[i]}' matrix.tsv
tasks.golden:
id	r1	r2
name	blue pen	red ink
qty	3	7
tasks.trap: awk's default FS splits on ANY whitespace, so `blue pen` becomes two fields and the transpose gains a phantom column — you must set FS to a literal tab (and join output cells with tabs, not OFS-default spaces). A build-string approach that appends `$i "\t"` unconditionally leaves a trailing tab on every line.

tasks.prompt:
The file scores.txt contains exactly these 7 lines:
eve 90
bob 85
alice 90
bob 92
carol 88
dave 92
eve 70
Each line is `name score`; names can repeat. First reduce to each player's MAXIMUM score, then print the top 3 players as `name score`, sorted by score DESCENDING with ties broken by name ASCENDING.
tasks.reference_command: awk '{if(!($1 in m) || $2>m[$1]) m[$1]=$2} END{for(n in m) print n, m[n]}' scores.txt | sort -k2,2nr -k1,1 | head -3
tasks.trap: Three stacked decisions: bob's max is his SECOND entry (92, not 85 — first-occurrence dedup like `sort -u -k1,1` or `!seen[$1]++` keeps 85); bob/dave tie at 92 and alice/eve tie at 90, so without an explicit `-k1,1` secondary key BSD sort's last-resort comparison under -r reverses the tie order; and the 90-tie sits exactly on the top-3 cutoff, so the wrong tie-break swaps eve in for alice.

tasks.prompt:
The file events.txt contains exactly these 5 lines:
03-Mar-2024 backup completed
15-Jan-2024 server rebooted
07-Feb-2024 disk replaced
15-Dec-2023 fleet audit done
28-Jan-2024 cache cleared
Each line starts with a date in DD-Mon-YYYY form (English 3-letter month) followed by a message. Reprint every line with the date converted to ISO YYYY-MM-DD form (2-digit month) followed by the unchanged message, and sort the output lines chronologically ascending.
tasks.setup:
printf '03-Mar-2024 backup completed
15-Jan-2024 server rebooted
07-Feb-2024 disk replaced
15-Dec-2023 fleet audit done
28-Jan-2024 cache cleared
' > events.txt
tasks.reference_command: awk 'BEGIN{split("Jan Feb Mar Apr May Jun Jul Aug Sep Oct Nov Dec",a," "); for(i=1;i<=12;i++) m[a[i]]=i} {split($1,d,"-"); msg=$0; sub(/^[^ ]+ /,"",msg); printf "%s-%02d-%s %s\n", d[3], m[d[2]], d[1], msg}' events.txt | sort
tasks.golden:
2023-12-15 fleet audit done
2024-01-15 server rebooted
2024-01-28 cache cleared
2024-02-07 disk replaced
2024-03-03 backup completed
tasks.trap: GNU `date -d '03-Mar-2024'` does not exist on BSD; BSD needs `date -j -f`, and reaching for the date binary at all invites locale/clock issues — the robust answer is a month-name lookup table in awk. Printing the month without %02d zero-padding (2024-3-03) or sorting before converting (Dec-2023 sorts after Mar-2024 lexically) both corrupt the order or bytes.

tasks.prompt:
The file access.log contains exactly these 7 lines:
10.0.0.1 - GET /index
172.16.4.2 - GET /a
10.0.0.1 - POST /b
192.168.1.10 - GET /c
10.0.0.1 - GET /d
172.16.4.2 - GET /e
192.168.1.10 - POST /f
Extract every IPv4 address (pattern: four 1-3 digit groups separated by dots) and print each distinct address with its occurrence count, formatted `ip count` (single space, ip first). Sort by count DESCENDING, ties by ip in ASCENDING lexicographic (string) order.
tasks.reference_command: grep -oE '[0-9]{1,3}(\.[0-9]{1,3}){3}' access.log | sort | uniq -c | sort -k1,1nr -k2,2 | awk '{print $2, $1}'
tasks.trap: grep -P and \d do not exist in BSD grep (must use -E with [0-9] classes). Leaving `uniq -c` output as-is fails on its leading space padding and count-first order — it must be reswizzled to `ip count`. And the 172/192 tie at count 2 requires an explicit `-k2,2` secondary key; last-resort comparison under a reversed numeric primary gets the tie backwards.

tasks.prompt:
The file orders.json contains exactly this JSON (one line):
[{"region":"east","items":[{"price":2.50,"qty":3},{"price":1.10,"qty":2}]},{"region":"west","items":[{"price":10.00,"qty":1}]},{"region":"east","items":[{"price":0.99,"qty":5}]},{"region":"north","items":[{"price":3.33,"qty":3}]}]
Compute total revenue per region (sum over all of that region's orders of price*qty over their items). Print one line per region formatted `region<TAB>total` with the total to exactly 2 decimal places (10 prints as 10.00), sorted by region name ASCENDING.
tasks.reference_command: jq -r '.[] | [.region, ([.items[] | .price*.qty] | add)] | @tsv' orders.json | awk -F'\t' '{s[$1]+=$2} END{for(r in s) printf "%s\t%.2f\n", r, s[r]}' | sort
tasks.trap: Doing the formatting inside jq is the killer: jq prints 10.00*1 as `10` (not `10.00`) and float sums can surface as 14.649999999999999; jq has no printf-style %.2f, and the `*100|round/100` idiom still prints `10` for west. The clean path hands raw sums to awk/printf for fixed 2-decimal formatting. `east` appears in two separate order objects and must be merged, and group_by/aggregation plus tab separators must all line up.

tasks.prompt:
You are given two CSV files in the current directory (headers on line 1):

products.csv columns: sku,cat
sales.csv columns: sku,qty,price

Compute per-category revenue where revenue for a sale line is (qty * price). If a sales.sku is not present in products.csv, attribute that line to category UNKNOWN. Categories that appear only in products.csv with no sales must NOT appear.

Output exactly one line per category that has at least one sale, format:
CAT|COUNT|REVENUE
where COUNT is the number of sale lines in that category (integer, no padding) and REVENUE is the sum of qty*price formatted with exactly two digits after the decimal point (awk/printf %.2f rules).

Sort lines by REVENUE descending (numeric), breaking ties by CAT ascending (lexicographic). Print a single trailing newline at end of output, nothing else. Use only the provided files; no network.
tasks.reference_command: awk -F, 'NR==FNR{if(NR>1)p[$1]=$2;next} FNR>1{c=($1 in p)?p[$1]:"UNKNOWN"; rev[c]+=$2*$3; n[c]++} END{for(c in rev) printf "%s|%d|%.2f\n", c, n[c], rev[c]}' products.csv sales.csv | sort -t'|' -k3,3nr -k1,1
tasks.trap: Missing UNKNOWN for orphan SKUs; sorting REVENUE lexicographically; forgetting qty*price; including categories with zero sales.

tasks.prompt:
File m.csv is a comma-separated table with a header. Lines may end with CRLF (\r\n). Columns: host,cpu,mem. The mem field may be empty.

Select rows where cpu >= 9.5 (numeric). Empty mem means 0.

Emit one line per selected host:
host:cpu:mem
where cpu is printed with exactly one digit after the decimal point (printf %.1f), and mem is a plain integer (no decimals, no padding).

Sort by cpu descending (numeric), then host ascending (pure lexicographic byte order — NOT natural/version sort). Single trailing newline. No extra spaces.
tasks.setup:
printf 'host,cpu,mem
' > m.csv
printf 'web1,80.5,
' >> m.csv
printf 'web2,9.5,1024
' >> m.csv
printf 'db1,80.5,2048
' >> m.csv
printf 'web10,100,512
' >> m.csv
printf 'api,9.50,256
' >> m.csv
tasks.trap: Leaving CR in fields; empty mem not coerced to 0; cpu as %.2f; natural sort so web2 before web10; treating 9.50 filter as string.

tasks.prompt:
File scores.csv has header team,user,score and one row per user score (integers).

For each team with at least 2 scores, compute the arithmetic mean of score.

Output lines:
TEAM AVG N
where AVG is mean formatted with exactly two decimal places (awk printf "%.2f" rounding), N is the integer count of scores for that team.

Omit teams with fewer than 2 scores. Sort by AVG descending (numeric), then N descending (numeric), then TEAM ascending (lexicographic). Single trailing newline.
tasks.reference_command: tail -n +2 scores.csv | awk -F, '{s[$1]+=$3;n[$1]++} END{for(t in s) if(n[t]>=2) printf "%s %.2f %d\n", t, s[t]/n[t], n[t]}' | sort -k2,2nr -k3,3nr -k1,1
tasks.trap: Wrong %.2f rounding for 26/3; including n<2 teams; sort key order AVG/N/TEAM wrong; integer division.

tasks.prompt:
File app.log has lines: DATE LEVEL where LEVEL is a lowercase word (error/info/warn/...).

Count occurrences of each LEVEL across the whole file (ignore DATE). Emit:
LEVEL COUNT
where LEVEL is uppercased and COUNT is the integer count zero-padded to width 3 (e.g. 006).

Sort by COUNT descending (numeric), then LEVEL ascending (lexicographic). Do not use the default spacing from `uniq -c` as your final format. Single trailing newline.
tasks.reference_command: awk '{c[$2]++} END{for(k in c) printf "%s %03d\n", toupper(k), c[k]}' app.log | sort -k2,2nr -k1,1
tasks.trap: Emitting uniq -c leading spaces as final format; forgetting toupper; zero-pad width wrong; sorting by level before count.

tasks.prompt:
Two parallel files (same number of lines, no headers):
words.txt — one word per line
need.txt — one integer per line

Keep a word only when length(word) is exactly equal to the integer on the same line number in need.txt.

Among kept words, count how many share each length L. Output:
L FREQ
where L and FREQ are unpadded integers.

Sort by FREQ descending (numeric), then L ascending (numeric). Single trailing newline. You will need a multi-stage pipeline.
tasks.reference_command: paste words.txt need.txt | awk '{if(length($1)==$2+0) print length($1)}' | sort -n | uniq -c | awk '{printf "%d %d\n",$2,$1}' | sort -k2,2nr -k1,1n
tasks.trap: Comparing length to need as strings; swapping L/FREQ columns; using uniq -c padding as output; sorting L before FREQ.

tasks.prompt:
File data.json is a single JSON object:
{"users":[{"id":...,"name":"...","tags":["..."]}, ...]}

For each distinct tag across all users, collect the names of users who have that tag. A user with an empty tags array contributes nothing.

Output one line per tag that appears at least once:
tag|count|names
where count is the number of users having the tag, and names is those users' names sorted ascending lexicographically and joined with a single comma and no spaces (e.g. Ann,Bob,Cy).

Sort lines by count descending (numeric), then tag ascending (lexicographic). Single trailing newline. jq is available.
tasks.reference_command: jq -r '.users[] | .name as $n | .tags[] | "\(.)\t\($n)"' data.json | sort -k1,1 -k2,2 | awk -F'\t' '{if($1!=prev&&prev!=""){printf "%s|%d|%s\n", prev, n, names; n=0; names=""} prev=$1; n++; names=(names==""?$2:names","$2)} END{if(prev!="")printf "%s|%d|%s\n",prev,n,names}' | sort -t'|' -k2,2nr -k1,1
tasks.trap: Spaces after commas in names; unsorted names; counting tag occurrences not users; including Dee empty tags; wrong secondary sort.

tasks.prompt:
Two headerless CSV files:
users.csv: id,name
bal.csv: id,amount

ids are opaque STRINGS (so "01" and "1" are different keys). Inner-join on id. Sum amount per name (if a name had multiple ids they would aggregate; here one id each). Drop ids present in only one file.

Output:
name total
where total is an unpadded integer sum of amount.

Sort by total descending (numeric), then name ascending (lexicographic). Single trailing newline.

Note: BSD join(1) requires both inputs sorted on the join key; unsorted join silently drops rows.
tasks.reference_command: join -t, <(sort -t, -k1,1 users.csv) <(sort -t, -k1,1 bal.csv) | awk -F, '{s[$2]+=$3} END{for(n in s) print n, s[n]}' | sort -k2,2nr -k1,1
tasks.trap: Coercing ids to numbers so 01==1; join without sort dropping rows; keeping unmatched id 3; wrong field after join.

tasks.prompt:
File text.txt contains English prose (may include punctuation and mixed case).

Normalize to lowercase. Split into words by treating every non-ASCII-letter run as a delimiter (only a-z remain as word characters). Discard words shorter than 4 characters. Count frequencies.

Emit the top 5 words as:
CC:word
where CC is the count zero-padded to width 2, and word is lowercase.

Order by count descending (numeric), then word ascending (lexicographic). If more than 5 words qualify after sorting, keep only the first 5. Single trailing newline. On macOS use BSD tr/sort/uniq (no GNU tr -s character-class surprises beyond what BSD supports).
tasks.setup:
printf 'The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. The fox was quick, very quick!\n' > text.txt
printf 'BROWN bears and brown foxes: quick thinking.\n' >> text.txt
tasks.reference_command: tr 'A-Z' 'a-z' < text.txt | tr -cs 'a-z' '\n' | awk 'length>=4' | sort | uniq -c | awk '{printf "%02d:%s\n",$1,$2}' | sort -t: -k1,1nr -k2,2 | head -5
tasks.trap: Keeping short words; failing to lowercase; punctuation glued to words; not zero-padding; wrong top-5 tie-break order.

note: v4 = 18 validated hard command-gen tasks (Fable Q1-Q10 + Grok G1-G8). All reference commands reproduce goldens. Adversarial cross-attempt: only Q5 stumped the opposing strong model (Grok); 17/18 solved one-shot by a frontier peer.
